{
  "name": "Fourier analytic method",
  "seminal_paper": {
    "arxiv_id": null,
    "doi": "10.1112/jlms/s1-28.1.104",
    "citation": "K. F. Roth, \"On certain sets of integers,\" Journal of the London Mathematical Society s1-28(1):104-109, 1953.",
    "year": 1953
  },
  "home_domain": "additive_combinatorics",
  "what_it_does": "The Fourier analytic method studies arithmetic progressions by representing a set through its indicator function on a finite additive group or interval. It converts additive-pattern questions into constraints on Fourier coefficients, enabling density-based existence or counting results for 3-term progressions.",
  "input_requirements": [
    "a finite additive ambient space such as [N] or Z/NZ containing the set under study",
    "a subset with positive density relative to that ambient space",
    "Fourier transform, Parseval, and convolution identities for the ambient additive group",
    "a goal expressible as existence or abundance of 3-term arithmetic progressions inside that subset"
  ],
  "input_match": [
    {
      "requirement": "a finite additive ambient space such as [N] or Z/NZ containing the set under study",
      "match": "PARTIAL",
      "evidence": "The target uses `Finset ℕ`, but no finite interval or cyclic-group ambient model is present."
    },
    {
      "requirement": "a subset with positive density relative to that ambient space",
      "match": "NO",
      "evidence": "no match"
    },
    {
      "requirement": "Fourier transform, Parseval, and convolution identities for the ambient additive group",
      "match": "NO",
      "evidence": "no match"
    },
    {
      "requirement": "a goal expressible as existence or abundance of 3-term arithmetic progressions inside that subset",
      "match": "PARTIAL",
      "evidence": "`Set.IsAPOfLength` records a 3-term arithmetic-progression condition, but the target asks finiteness of consecutive powerful-number triples rather than density-forced existence."
    }
  ],
  "known_obstructions": [
    "Powerful numbers have natural density zero, so classical Roth hypotheses do not apply directly.",
    "The target concerns consecutive terms of the powerful-number sequence, adding an order-gap constraint absent from standard Roth settings.",
    "A finiteness conclusion for exceptional consecutive triples is structurally different from a density-based existence theorem."
  ],
  "fit_score": 0.25,
  "source_analog_id": "A4"
}
